evidence: Academies have their local towns, regions, catchment areas locked the fuck down. And that is why you can reliably generate talent. If you aren't able to do that, you don't survive as an academy.
Ivor immediately tracked ('yeah, 100%') on V's territorial lock-in thesis for talent pipelines.

Ivor's framing of 'career notebook from first job to retirement' connects to V's cornerstone that trust relationships compound over time.

I've become so black pilled on the idea that we are so limited by the ATS and the realities and the practical limits of how we process talent at scale that it's hard to even conceive of a world where you could have actual insight into how someone thinks before talking to them.
Companies will never invest in true AI for resume scanning. Because why would you process a resource where the ratio is like 99 crap for 1% signal. And even that could be totally fake now.

What we are is talent scanning, and that's literally the only thing that matters. That is the only problem we actually solve.
Ilse acknowledged this was a 'full face pivot' from how she'd been positioning the product even in calls 'yesterday and the day before.'

source_display: The Quiet Part Problem as Market Opportunity
evidence: Employers have selection criteria they cannot legally state. The gap between stated job requirements and actual selection preferences is a structural feature of hiring markets, not a bug.

source_display: Response Rate as Only Metric That Matters
evidence: If Careerspan's candidates respond at higher rates than other sources, that's sufficient proof of value. Use Careerspan's high response rates as the proxy metric for convincing recruiters.
An Indian recruiter placing US engineers said: 'I have nothing to give these people besides the resume. All of these people look the same.'
The hiring market has become so information-efficient that traditional recruiter differentiation (sourcing, resume parsing, outreach) is commoditized.

I wouldn't think of it as a safety blanket. I would think of it as demonstrating—our demonstrating our worth upfront... it gets these recruiters that have been burned by software and that are not super trusting of AI to see that it does, in fact, generate some names worth paying attention to.

In talent marketplaces, having the name of a prestigious firm on your website may be worth more than their actual business contribution. Superposition wouldn't bring users... But their logo creates a trust cascade.
Everything you just said about Superposition is also true about working with a recruiter specifically. Except we don't get to put Superposition on our website.
When you tell them yes, but we can make sure that it's all top 20 users from their alumni associations... Literally no one in the world can offer that.
This inverts the network effects playbook. Instead of 'we're valuable because we're big,' it's 'we're valuable because we're exclusive.'
If I were to stack rank the moments of 'oh shit, this is really useful to me' from a recruiter perspective, it would be: I'm seeing people that would have taken me a while to find.
Logan's biotech friend disrupted her industry by going directly to morgues instead of through middlemen. 'My middleman is charging me too much money' → go direct to source.
